title: Coding Assignment 1
text:
Photography is something I like to do during my free time. I like taking photos of food, people, pets and everyday moments.


I enjoy photography because it lets me be creative and expressive.




Food
Nature and landcapes
People
My dog and cat

Fujifilm X-M5
Canon EOS R8
Canon Ae-1




I learned how to do this from: Quarto






Photo of my cat





We can use mean() to calculate averages.



age <- c(17, 19, 20, 25, 30) mean(age)

title: Coding Assignment 2
text:
Problem 2: Clean the data
For problem 2, I cleaned the data as required. In the temperature file, I removed those rows that are -9999. AvgTemp is stored as “hundredths of a degree”, so I divided by 100 to get real °C. Also, I created a country code column from the first two letters of the station ID. Then I removed duplicate country codes so each code only shows once.


[1] 272


# A tibble: 0 × 2
# ℹ 2 variables: FIPS 10-4 <chr>, n <int>

title: Coding Assignment 2
text:
Problem 3: Set parameters
For problem 3, I set my own parameters country, year begin, year end and month. This makes it easy to change the query without rewriting the whole code.


# A tibble: 1 × 5
  data_file     country year_begin year_end month
  <chr>         <chr>        <dbl>    <dbl> <dbl>
1 my_output.csv Japan         2000     2025     1

title: Coding Assignment 2
text:
Problem 4: Join + Filter (Japan)
For Problem 4, I joined the datasets and then filtered the data using my parameters. I selected the required columns, sorted by station NAME, and showed the first 7 and last 7 rows. Finally, I saved the result as a CSV file.

title: Assignment 2
text:
Problem 5: Another filter (Algeria)

data_file2  <- "algeria_june_2000_2025.csv"
country2    <- "Algeria"
year_begin2 <- 2000
year_end2   <- 2025
month2      <- 6

result2 <- all_data_export |>
  filter(
    Name == country2,
    Year >= year_begin2,
    Year <= year_end2,
    Month == month2
  ) |>
  select(
    NAME, LATITUDE, LONGITUDE,
    Country = Name,
    Year, Month, AvgTemp
  ) |>
  arrange(NAME)

head(result2, 7)

# A tibble: 7 × 7
  NAME  LATITUDE LONGITUDE Country  Year Month AvgTemp
  <chr>    <dbl>     <dbl> <chr>   <dbl> <dbl>   <dbl>
1 ADRAR     27.9     -0.28 Algeria  2000     6    34.3
2 ADRAR     27.9     -0.28 Algeria  2001     6    35.6
3 ADRAR     27.9     -0.28 Algeria  2003     6    35.3
4 ADRAR     27.9     -0.28 Algeria  2004     6    33.8
5 ADRAR     27.9     -0.28 Algeria  2005     6    34.4
6 ADRAR     27.9     -0.28 Algeria  2006     6    35.1
7 ADRAR     27.9     -0.28 Algeria  2007     6    34.1

tail(result2, 7)

# A tibble: 7 × 7
  NAME   LATITUDE LONGITUDE Country  Year Month AvgTemp
  <chr>     <dbl>     <dbl> <chr>   <dbl> <dbl>   <dbl>
1 ZENATA     35.0     -1.45 Algeria  2019     6    22.1
2 ZENATA     35.0     -1.45 Algeria  2020     6    23.2
3 ZENATA     35.0     -1.45 Algeria  2021     6    23.0
4 ZENATA     35.0     -1.45 Algeria  2022     6    24.6
5 ZENATA     35.0     -1.45 Algeria  2023     6    24.4
6 ZENATA     35.0     -1.45 Algeria  2024     6    22.9
7 ZENATA     35.0     -1.45 Algeria  2025     6    26.7

write_csv(result2, data_file2)

title: Assignment 2
Assignment 2
text:
Problem 7: Plot

summary_year_long <- summary_year |>
  pivot_longer(
    cols = c(mean_temp, min_temp, max_temp),
    names_to = "stat",
    values_to = "temp"
  )

ggplot(summary_year_long, aes(x = Year, y = temp, color = stat)) +
  geom_line() +
  labs(
    title = "Temperature Summary by Year",
    x = "Year",
    y = "Temperature (°C)",
    color = "Statistic"
  )

title: Coding Assignment 2
text:
Problem 5: Summaries
For Problem 5, I summarized the joined data. I made three tables: summary_year, coverage_year, and coverage_country. Then I reported the number of measurements in 2025 and the top 5 countries overall.


# A tibble: 1 × 2
   Year n_measurements
  <dbl>          <int>
1  2025         126155


# A tibble: 5 × 2
  Name          n_measurements
  <chr>                  <int>
1 United States        7810379
2 Canada               1540423
3 Russia                788220
4 Australia             760851
5 Germany               518642


# A tibble: 1 × 2
  min_year max_year
     <dbl>    <dbl>
1     1701     2026

title: Coding Assignment 2
text:
Problem 6: Plot
For Problem 6, I made a line plot using the yearly summary table. It shows the mean, min, and max temperature over time.
